questions.question: What is the main purpose of risk assessment in health and social care?
options: To create as much paperwork as possible for auditors
To make sure staff are blamed when accidents happen
To stop people who use services from doing anything at all
To spot what could cause harm and act before it does

questions.question: How is a hazard best defined?
options: The chance that someone will actually be harmed
Anything with the potential to cause harm
The seriousness of an injury once it has occurred
A written record of precautions in place

questions.question: How is a risk best defined?
options: A legal document signed off by a manager
The source of possible harm, such as a hot drink
The chance that harm will occur, together with how serious it could be
Any piece of equipment used in a care setting

questions.question: Which piece of legislation places a general duty on employers to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety and welfare of workers and others affected by the work?
options: The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999
The Care Standards Act 2000
The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
The Mental Capacity Act 2005

questions.question: Which regulations make it an explicit legal requirement for every employer to carry out a suitable and sufficient assessment of risks?
options: The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999
The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
The Equality Act 2010
The Data Protection Act 2018

questions.question: Under the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, when must the significant findings of an assessment be recorded?
options: Only in hospitals and care homes
Never — recording is entirely optional
Where five or more people are employed
Only when an accident has already happened

questions.question: Which of these is a worker's own legal duty under health and safety law?
options: To remove every possible hazard before starting any task
To write and sign off the service's formal risk assessments
To decide alone what is reasonably practicable for the whole service
To take reasonable care, follow safe systems and training, use equipment properly and report hazards

questions.question: The HSE sets out a practical approach to assessing risk that is often described as how many steps?
options: Three steps
Five steps
Seven steps
Ten steps

questions.question: What is the first of the HSE five steps to risk assessment?
options: Review and update the assessment
Record your findings
Identify the hazards
Provide personal protective equipment

questions.question: What is the second of the HSE five steps?
options: Decide who might be harmed and how
Evaluate the risks and decide on controls
Identify the hazards
Record your findings

questions.question: Which of these is a good source of information when identifying hazards (Step 1)?
options: Waiting until an injury occurs before writing anything down
Guessing what might go wrong without looking
Accident and incident records, manufacturers' instructions and safety data sheets
Only the opinion of the newest member of staff

questions.question: When deciding who might be harmed (Step 2), what should you do?
options: Assume everyone faces exactly the same level of risk
Identify the groups at risk and be clear about how harm might happen
Name every individual person in the building by full name
Only consider the staff and ignore the people you support

questions.question: Which description of harm best reflects the course's guidance for Step 2?
options: "There is a hazard here"
"Someone could slip on the wet floor and fracture a hip"
"The floor is wet"
"Be careful"

questions.question: When evaluating a risk (Step 3), which two factors should you weigh up?
options: How likely harm is and how serious it would be
The number of staff on duty and the time of day only
The person's postcode and their date of birth
The cost of equipment and the age of the building

questions.question: What does reducing risk "so far as is reasonably practicable" mean?
options: Balancing the level of risk against the time, trouble and cost of controlling it
Removing every conceivable hazard whatever the cost
Leaving all decisions about risk to chance on the day
Doing nothing unless an accident has already happened

questions.question: What is Step 4 of the HSE five steps?
options: Record your findings
Provide personal protective equipment
Identify the hazards
Decide who might be harmed and how

questions.question: Which best describes a useful written record of a risk assessment?
options: As long and detailed as possible, listing every trivial thing
Something only the manager is ever allowed to read
A form filed away and never looked at again
Clear enough for any worker to follow, kept where staff can find it, with a plan for anything still to fix

questions.question: What is Step 5 of the HSE five steps?
options: Identify the hazards
Evaluate the risks and decide on controls
Review and update the assessment
Decide who might be harmed

questions.question: Which of these is a trigger for reviewing a risk assessment?
options: Nothing at all should ever prompt a review
A person's condition or mobility changes, or an accident or near miss occurs
Only a request from an external inspector
Only the passing of exactly ten years

questions.question: In the hierarchy of control, which measure is the most reliable and effective?
options: Wearing gloves and an apron
Eliminating the hazard completely
Personal protective equipment
Putting up a warning sign

questions.question: Where does personal protective equipment (PPE) sit in the hierarchy of control?
options: At the top, as the most effective control
It is not part of the hierarchy at all
In the middle, above safe systems of work
At the bottom, as the last line of defence

questions.question: Why is PPE placed at the bottom of the hierarchy of control?
options: Because PPE is never useful in care settings
Because it is the most effective way to control any risk
Because it protects only the wearer, only when worn correctly, and does not remove the hazard
Because only managers are allowed to decide when it is used

questions.question: What is a safe system of work?
options: A list of everyone employed at the service
A legal Act of Parliament
A piece of personal protective equipment
An agreed way of carrying out a task, designed to remove or reduce risk

questions.question: According to the course, what should you do if a safe system cannot be followed because equipment is missing or broken?
options: Skip the task and tell no one
Find an unofficial workaround and carry on
Use a single-use item several times to save time
Stop and report it rather than working around it

questions.question: Who is responsible for carrying out and signing off a service's formal risk assessment?
options: A manager or other competent person
The people who use the service
Any care worker, on their own judgement
Visitors and family members

questions.question: A hazard and a risk mean exactly the same thing.
options: True
False

questions.question: Risk assessment is not just good practice — it is a legal requirement.
options: True
False

questions.question: The aim of a risk assessment is to record every trivial thing and wrap people in cotton wool.
options: True
False

questions.question: When deciding who might be harmed, a good assessment considers people who face greater risk from the same hazard, such as someone living with dementia or a new worker.
options: True
False

questions.question: A risk assessment is a form that should be filed once and then forgotten.
options: True
False

questions.question: Measures at the top of the hierarchy of control protect people automatically and do not depend on individuals remembering to do the right thing.
options: True
False

questions.question: Dynamic risk assessment is a licence to abandon the written plan and take chances.
options: True
False

questions.question: Where an adult has the mental capacity to make a decision, they are entitled to make choices that others might see as unwise.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of hazards in a care setting? (Select all that apply)
options: Cleaning products or medicines
A heavy load to be moved
A trailing cable
A hot drink
A properly stored, unopened first aid kit sitting safely in a cupboard

questions.question: Under health and safety law, which duties fall on the worker? (Select all that apply)
options: Take reasonable care of themselves and others
Use equipment properly
Report hazards and concerns
Follow the safe systems and training provided
Personally write and sign off the service's formal risk assessments

questions.question: Which of the following are among the HSE five steps to risk assessment? (Select all that apply)
options: Decide who might be harmed and how
Evaluate the risks and decide on controls
Charge the person a fee for the assessment
Identify the hazards
Record your findings

questions.question: Which groups of people might be harmed and should be considered in a care risk assessment? (Select all that apply)
options: Only the manager who signs the assessment
Visitors, family and contractors
People you support
Staff
Members of the public

questions.question: Which of the following belong to the hierarchy of control? (Select all that apply)
options: Reduce and isolate the risk
Eliminate the hazard
Personal protective equipment as the last line
Ignore the hazard until an inspector visits
Control the risk through safe systems of work

questions.question: Which of the following are events or changes that should trigger a review of a risk assessment? (Select all that apply)
options: A person's needs or mobility changes
You notice a control is no longer working in practice
Nothing has changed and everyone is content
An accident or near miss occurs
New equipment or a new task is introduced

questions.question: According to the course, what is a care worker responsible for in making controls work? (Select all that apply)
options: Using the equipment provided as intended
Being the eyes and ears of the service
Reporting promptly when a control is missing, broken or no longer fits the person
Deciding alone what is reasonably practicable for the whole service
Following the agreed safe systems reliably

questions.question: A ___ is anything with the potential to cause harm.
The Health and Safety at Work etc. ___ 1974 places a general duty on employers to protect the health, safety and welfare of workers and others.
The most reliable control in the hierarchy is to ___ the hazard completely; PPE is the last line of defence.
The quick, continuous judgement you make on the spot as a situation unfolds is called ___ risk assessment.
Working with a person to weigh the benefits of an activity against the risks, rather than saying no by default, is known as positive ___.
